one_line_takeaway: A conceptual framework that ties SOC AI autonomy (a 5-level SAE-J3016-style scale) to human oversight and calibrated trust via simple scalar equations, illustrated by a single fine-tuned-LLM cyber-range case study reporting large but non-rigorously-validated before/after gains (false positives 70->35%, MTTR 8h->90min).
key_finding: In the ACDC cyber-range wargames, the CyberAlly-augmented SOC produced substantial operational gains over the pre-CyberAlly manual baseline: AI-driven triage halved false positives from 70% to 35%; investigation time for a conveyor-belt MITM incident dropped from ~3 hours to ~1 hour (a 67% reduction) via automated log correlation and MITRE ATT&CK mapping; Mean Time To Respond (MTTR) fell from 8 hours to 90 minutes for isolate/block actions via SOAR; automated ticketing rose from 10% to 75%; and overall response times were cut by over 60%. Most incidents in Events 3-4 were triaged/contained within 20-40 minutes. Qualitatively, trust was 'earned' through a graduated autonomy escalation (Level 1 full-HITL -> Level 2 on-the-loop -> Level 3 conditional), reducing analyst alert fatigue while keeping humans in control of critical/novel decisions.
core_contribution: A conceptual/organizational framework (not a new algorithm or model) for structuring Human-AI collaboration in Security Operations Centers (SOCs). The paper's own contribution has two parts. (1) A 'triadic' formal model linking three pillars: Autonomy A, Human-in-the-Loop involvement H, and Trust T, expressed as toy scalar equations A = 1 - (lambda1*C + lambda2*R)(1-T) [complexity C, risk R, trust T], H = 1 - A, and T = alpha1*E + alpha2*P + alpha3*(1-U) [explainability E, performance-history P, uncertainty U]. (2) A five-level SOC autonomy scale (Level 0 manual to Level 4 fully autonomous, with numeric A ranges e.g. L0~0, L1~0.2-0.4, L2~0.4-0.6, L3~0.7-0.8, L4~0.9-1.0), explicitly adapted from the SAE J3016 driving-automation standard, mapped onto NIST CSF functions (Identify/Protect/Detect/Respond/Recover/Govern), four SOC analyst tiers, and three human-oversight modes (Human-in-Control / Human-on-the-Loop / Human-out-of-the-Loop). The framework is demonstrated via a case study ('CyberAlly'/'AI-Avatar'), a fine-tuned GPT-based LLM SOC assistant with RAG and knowledge graphs, run in the ACDC cyber-range. NOTE for the KG: the A2C (Automate-Augment-Collaborate) framework, the RHML reciprocal-learning model, the CHT-DQN cognitive-hierarchy RL framework, and the SOC Automation Matrix are CITED related work (Chhetri, Cohen, Aref, Tilbury/Flowerday respectively) in Section 5, NOT contributions of this paper.
threat_model: The paper is a collaboration/governance framework, not an attack-defense paper, so it does not define a formal adversary or threat model of its own. The operational adversary context it targets is the day-to-day SOC problem of alert overload and slow response (cited figures: ~4,484 alerts/day, up to 99% false positives, 44% rise in 2024 cyberattacks, 277-day average breach identify+contain time). The case study supplies a concrete red-vs-blue adversary: a Red Team (4 members) attacking a simulated maritime port IT/OT environment (55 VMs) across three scenarios: (1) false-data injection on IoT depth sensors, (2) PLC/HMI/SCADA manipulation of an ore-loading conveyor belt, (3) disruption of an autonomous train supply-chain system, with an attack chain of IT phishing -> reverse shell -> lateral movement -> OT compromise (mapped to MITRE ATT&CK / cyber kill chain). This is a demonstration scenario, not a claimed security guarantee against a defined attacker.
limitations: (1) Evidence is a single, self-constructed simulated case study in one cyber range; the headline metrics (70->35% FP, 3h->1h, 8h->90min, 10->75% ticketing, >60% response cut) are reported before/after with no baseline system, no control group, no statistical validation, no confidence intervals, and no reproducibility artifacts, so they read as illustrative rather than rigorously established. (2) The formal A/H/T equations are simple hand-specified scalar formulas; the weight parameters (lambda, alpha) and the inputs (complexity, risk, explainability, performance, uncertainty) are never operationally measured, calibrated, or empirically fit, so the model is a conceptual scaffold rather than a validated quantitative instrument. (3) H = 1 - A forces a strict inverse coupling of oversight and autonomy that may be an oversimplification. (4) Governance (NIST CSF 2.0 Govern) is explicitly declared out of scope. (5) The environment is synthetic (maritime-port IT/OT wargame, 55 VMs); real-world SOC generalization, adversarial robustness of the LLM, and hallucination/error rates are asserted (RAG+KG 'minimize errors') but not quantified. (6) 'Misclassifications were rare' is stated without numbers.

datasets_used: ACDC (Augmenting Cyber Defence Capability) cyber-range wargame data: two years of simulated cyber incidents plus data from five ACDC cyber-defense exercises (each with 4 Red Team and 10 Blue Team members)

Simulated maritime-port hybrid IT-OT 'ACDC Port' environment: 55 VMs spanning business, public services, IoT devices, and ICS logistics; three attack scenarios (IoT depth sensors, ore-loading conveyor belt PLC/HMI/SCADA, autonomous train supply chain)
RAG knowledge base: MITRE ATT&CK tactics, threat-intelligence feeds, network topology, and knowledge graphs of hosts/users/vulnerabilities/incidents (no public benchmark dataset used)
quantitative_results: False positives halved from 70% to 35% via AI-driven triage
Investigation time reduced from ~3 hours to ~1 hour (67% improvement) for the conveyor-belt MITM incident
MTTR reduced from 8 hours to 90 minutes for isolate-endpoint / block-malicious-IP actions via SOAR
Overall response times cut by over 60%
Event 3 incidents mostly triaged/mitigated within 20-40 minutes; Event 4 resolutions clustered below 40 minutes
Autonomy scale numeric ranges: Level 0 A~0; Level 1 A~0.2-0.4; Level 2 A~0.4-0.6; Level 3 A~0.7-0.8; Level 4 A~0.9-1.0

Cited motivating stats (not this paper's measurements): ~4,484 alerts/day, 67% of alerts ignored, up to 99% false positives; average 277 days (204 to identify + 73 to contain) per IBM 2024; 44% rise in 2024 cyberattacks; projected $23T cybercrime damage by 2027
baselines_compared: Pre-CyberAlly manual SOC baseline: the quantitative gains are all before/after comparisons against manual/pre-integration operation, not against a competing AI system

Qualitative comparison only (Table 5) against four prior frameworks: Tilbury & Flowerday (SOC Automation Matrix / LOA, Levels 0-3), Chhetri et al. A2C (Levels 1-3), Cohen et al. RHML reciprocal learning (Levels 1-2), Aref et al. CHT-DQN (Level 3); the paper claims its own work is the only one comprehensively covering all four SOC functions plus Autonomy+Trust+HITL+SA
No quantitative baseline, no ablation, and no head-to-head evaluation against any other system
